Econ 605 Notes

Parker Howell

Last Updated: 2024-05-28

1 Macro Variables, Capital, and Wealth

1.0.1 “GDP”

\[Y = C + I + G + NX\]

1.0.2 Def “Deflating Nominal Variables”

\[X_t = \frac{X_t^{nom}}{P_{X,t}}\] where \(P_X\) is the deflator or price index which converts base year dollars into \(t\)-year dollars for variable \(X\).

Must deflate \(Y^{nom}, C^{nom}, I^{nom}\) with the same price index to maintain additive relationship between real and nominal variables.

Typically choose \(p_{C,t}\):

Given \(Y_t^{nom} = C_t^{nom} + I_t^{nom}\), we have that \(Y_t = C_t + I_t \iff Y_t = \frac{Y_t^{nom}}{p_{C, t}}, C_t = \frac{C_t^{nom}}{p_{C, t}}\), and \(I_t = \frac{I_t^{nom}}{p_{C, t}}\)

Unless otherwise stated: \(K \neq \frac{K^{nom}}{p_I}\). Instead: \(K = \frac{K^{nom}}{p_C} = \frac{p_I}{p_C} X\)

1.0.3 “\(p_X\)”

\[p_X \equiv \frac{P_I}{P_C} = \text{price of capital} = \frac{apples}{bricks}\] So, \[K = \frac{P_I}{P_C}X = p_X X\]

1.0.4 Def “Stocks, Flows, and Rates”

  • Stocks: “dollars variables”
    • e.g., \(A, K, X\)
  • Flows: “dollars per year”
    • e.g., \(Y, C, I\)
  • Rates: “inverse time”
    • e.g., \(d, r, g_X \equiv \frac{\frac{dX}{dt}}{X}\)

1.0.5 “Dollars, Apples, and Bricks; Units of Measurement”

  • Dollars: current year, or nominal, dollars; units of \(Y^{nom}, C^{nom}, I^{nom}\)
  • Apples: base year dollars; units of real \(Y, C, I\), and wealth, \(K, A\)
  • Bricks: base year investment dollars; units of \(X, \frac{I^{nom}}{P_I}\), where \(P_I\) is an investment price index that tracks inflation in new capital goods.

Units of Measurement

  • Prices
    • Consumer Price Index: \(P_C\): \(\frac{\$}{apples}\)
    • Investment Price Index: \(P_I\): \(\frac{\$}{bricks}\)
  • Stocks
    • Wealth: \(K, A\): apples
    • Capital (quantity): \(X\): bricks
  • Flows (Nominal and Real):
    • \(Y^{nom}, C^{nom}, I^{nom}\): \(\frac{\$}{year}\)
    • \(Y, C, I\): \(\frac{apple}{year}\)

1.0.6 “Wealth”

Wealth is the quantity of apples the households can currently buy if they sell everything they own.

Nominal wealth: \[K^{nom} = P_I * X\]

Real wealth is the number of apples that can be bought with nominal wealth: \[K = \frac{K^{nom}}{P_C} = \frac{P_I}{P_C} X\]

\(p_X = \frac{p_I}{p_C}\)

1.0.7 “\(K\) vs \(X\)”

  • $K = $ market value of capital
  • $X = $ quantity of capital
  • \(K = P_X * X = \frac{P_I}{P_C} X\)

1.0.8 Def “Capital Accumulation Equation”

Discrete: \[K_{t+1} - K_t = I_t - dK_t = I_t - (\delta - g_{p,t})K_t\] Continuous: \[\dot{K}_t = I_t - d K_t\] Sometimes given as: \[\dot{X}_t = q_t I_t - \delta X_t\] where \(q_t\) is given a functional form

1.0.9 “Depreciation rate”

\[d \equiv \underbrace{\delta}_{\text{wear + tear}} - \underbrace{g_p}_{\text{capital gain}} = \text{depreciation rate on MARKET VALUE of capital}\]

1.0.10 “Average growth rate”

CAUTION: don’t just do: \(\frac{332 - 100}{100} = 2.23\%\)!

1.0.11 “Relationship between capital stock and past investment history”

Market value of capital (\(K\)) is the sum of undepreciated parts of past real investment expenditures. I.e., \[K_{t+1} = \sum_{\tau \geq 0} I_{t-\tau} (1-d)^\tau\]

1.0.12 “Household Wealth”

\[A_t = K_t + D_t\] where

  • \(A_t\) = household financial assets at date \(t\)
  • \(K_t\) = market value of private business assets
  • \(D_t\) = market value of government debt

1.0.13 “Debt Accumulation Equation”

\[D_{t+1} - D_t = \underbrace{r_t D_t}_{\text{interest due}} + \underbrace{(G_t - T_t)}_{\text{primary deficit}}\] * In-flow: \(r_t D_t + G_t\) * Stock: \(B_t\) * Out-flow: \(T_t\)

1.0.14 “Wealth Accumulation Equation”

\[\begin{align} A_{t+1} - A_t &= K_{t+1} - K_t + D_{t+1} - D_t \\ &=I_t - dK_t + r_t D_t + (G_t - T_t) \\ &= Y_t - C_t -dK_t + r_t D_t - T_t\\ &= \underbrace{Y_t + r_t D_t}_{\text{inflow}} - \underbrace{(C_t + T_t + dK_t)}_{\text{outflow}} \end{align}\]

  • Stock: \(A_t = K_t + D_t\)

Government debt is a form of wealth

2 Solow

  • \(\dot{A_t}\): (flow) wealth??

3 OLG

  • ??

4 Ramsey

4.1 Planner’s Problem

  • \(H\): total number of households
  • \(l_t\): household size (exogenous)
  • \(L_t = 1 *l_t * H\) (1 = labor supply per person)
  • \(C_t = Hx_t\)
  • \(x_t\): household consumption - CONTROL! (state is \(K\)) but we replace this with:
  • \(c_t = \frac{C_t}{e^{g_t}L_t}\)
  • \(k_t = \frac{K_t}{e^{g_t}L_t}\)
  • \(\rho\): time discounting (impatience)
  • \(\theta\): IES intertemporal elasticity of substitution
  • \(\theta=-{\frac {u_{c}(c)}{c\cdot u_{cc}(c)}}=-{\frac {d\ln c}{d\ln(u'(c))}}\)
  • \(g_Y = n + g\)
  • \(\frac{\dot{c}}{c}+g\): growth rate of per-capita consumption
  • \(\vec{\alpha} = (\rho, \theta, n, d, g)\) ALL ARE EXOGENOUS
  • \(PD(\vec{\alpha})\): phase diagram for comparative dynamics

From Optimal Control Slides:

  • \(\bar{k}\): max debt constraint or min wealth constraint IN TERMINAL STATE
  • \(\Gamma\): law of motion for capital/wealth/the state variable: \(\Gamma(k_t,c_t,t) = \dot{k}\)
  • \(\lambda_t\): marginal benefit of one unit of \(k_t\)
  • \(\lambda_t k_t\) = agent’s wealth measured in units of utility (i.e., potential to receive future utility)

LOM for capital: \[\dot{K} = I - dK = Y - C - dK\]

LOM for capital, intensive form: \[\dot{k_t} = y_t - c_t - (n+d)k_t = \underbrace{f(k_t) - c_t}_{\substack{\text{actual investment} \\ \text{per effective worker}}} - \underbrace{(n+d+g)k_t}_{\substack{\text{replacement investment} \\ \text{per effective worker}}}\]

Production Function/Total Output \[Y = F(K, e^{gt}L) = C_t + I_t\]

4.1.1 Planner’s Problem

\[\underbrace{V(k_0, t)}_{\text{indirect utility}} = \max_{c_t} \int_t^\infty e^{\rho t}e^{nt} u(c_t)dt \text{ s to}\]

  1. \(\dot{k} = y_t - c_t - (n+d)k_t\)
  2. \(k_0 >0\) given
  3. \(\lim_{t \rightarrow \infty} \lambda_t k_t\) (transversality condition with exponential discounting)
  4. implicitly: \(k_t, c_t \geq 0\) - will never bind due to Inada so we usually ignore them

Planner’s Problem FOCs:

  • \(\frac{\partial \mathcal H}{\partial c_t} = 0\)
  • \(\frac{\partial \mathcal H}{\partial k_t} = -\dot{\lambda}\)
  • \(\frac{\partial \mathcal H}{\partial \lambda_t} = \dot{k_t}\)

4.1.2 Euler Equation for Consumption (Solution to the Ramsey Problem)

\[\frac{\dot{c_t}}{c_t}=\frac{1}{\theta}(r_t- (\rho + \theta g ))\]

Phase Diagram Equations (steady state)

\[\dot{k} = 0 \iff c_t = f(k_t) - (n + d + g)k_t\] \[\dot{c} = 0 \iff k_t = k_*, \text{ where } f'(k_*) = d + \rho + \theta g\] \[c_* = f(k_*) - (n+d+g)k_*\]

4.1.3 Golden Rule vs Steady State Capital

  • \(f'(k_*) = d + \rho + \theta g\)
  • \(f'(k_G) = d + n + g\)

So \[f'(k_*) > f'(k_G) \iff \rho > n + (1-\theta)g\]

4.1.4 Isoclines

  • \(\dot{k} = 0\): \(c_t = f(k_t) - (n+d+g)k_t\)
  • \(\dot{c} = 0\): \(r = \rho + \theta g\) or \(k_t=k_*\), where \(f'(k_*)=d+\rho + \theta g\)

Usually, \(g = 0\), especially if you get the EE from the consumer max problem.

\(\lambda\) never jumps at \(t_A\) (unless capital is destroyed which is rare) \(c\) always jumps at \(t_A\) (i.e., when there is a surprise). We don’t know which way it jumps without more clues though.

4.1.5 Algorithm to solve Ramsey Shocks

  1. Transition \(K\) to \(k\)
  2. Solve Planner’s Problem
    1. Derive EE
    2. Check that you have two DEs (should look like this):
      1. \(\frac{\dot{c_t}}{c_t} = \alpha k^{\alpha - 1} - d - \rho\)
      2. \(\dot{k_t} = k_t^\alpha - c_t - (n+d)k_t\)
  3. Derive isoclines. How do they move (if at all)?
    1. \(\dot{c} = 0 \Rightarrow k = (\frac{\alpha}{d + \rho})^{\frac{1}{1-\alpha}}\) (hint: make sure exponent is positive)
    2. \(\dot{k} = 0 \Rightarrow c_t = k_t^\alpha - (n+d)k_t\)
  4. Is there a jump in \(c_t\) at \(t_c\)?
    1. Capital not destroyed, so co-state is continuous
    2. Set \(\lambda_{t-0} = \lambda_{t+0}\) to see if \(c_t\) is continuous or not.
  5. Draw diagram
    1. old/new curves + old/new arrows
    2. where should you be at \(t_{c-0}\) to be able the new stable arm?
    3. what is plausible behavior at \(t=t_A\)? Options are
      1. stay
      2. jump up
      3. jump down (can’t always choose which one–sometimes either 2 or 3 is correct in drawings)
    4. Label jumps and their timing

4.2 Ramsey - Competitive Equilibrium/Household Expectations

  • \(c_{it}\): per-capita consumption (not consumption per effective worker)
  • \(g\): tech growth. We usually assume \(g = 0\) - no technology growth in the consumer problem so the Euler equations of the Household and Planner coincide.
  • \(\varepsilon_u\): elasticity of Marginal Utility growth with respect to consumption growth. If \(\varepsilon_u\) rises, then consumption growth is less sensitive to changes in the interest rate. In other words, consumers prefer smooth consumption despite time-varying interest rates.
  • \(\Phi(K):\) an expectation/belief function that allows households to forecast future factor prices

4.2.1 Household Utility Maximization

\[\max_{c_{it}} \int_0^\infty e^{-\rho t} L_{it}u(c_{it})dt \text{ s. to: }\]

  1. \(\dot{A_{it}} = r_tA_{it} + w_t L_t - c_{it}L_{it}\) (aka household flow budget constraint) DON’T USE \(\dot{K}\) here!!
  2. \(A_{i0}\): given
  3. \(\lim_{T \rightarrow \infty} e^{-\bar{r}(0,T)}A_{it}\geq 0\)
  • Individual State Variable: \(A_{it}\)
  • Aggregate state variables: can use \(r_t, w_t\) or \(K_t, L_t\). Both will give the same answer, but we use \(K_t, L_t\) in this class.

Household’s Utility Maximization Problem FOCs:

  • \(\frac{\partial \mathcal H}{\partial c_{it}} = 0 \iff\)
  • \(\frac{\partial \mathcal H}{\partial A_{it}} = -\dot{\mu_{it}}\)

Then eliminate \(\mu_t\), do some nasty rearranging and define \(\varepsilon_u = |\frac{u''(c_{it})c_{it}}{u'(c_{it})}|\)

4.2.2 Euler Equation for Consumption (Solution to the Ramsey Problem)

\[\frac{\dot{c_{it}}}{c_{it}}=\frac{1}{\varepsilon_u}(r_t- \rho) = -\frac{u'(c_t)}{u''(c_t)}(r_t - \rho)\]

This is identical to the Euler equation in the planner’s problem if \(g = 0\) and \(\theta = \varepsilon_u\).

4.2.3 Ramsey Asset Market Clearing

\[A_t = K_t\] Technically also have the capital leasing sector which requires \[A_D = K_t \text{ and } L_D = L_t\]

4.2.3.1 Ramsey Capital leasing max problem

Idk if this is important, so don’t get sidetracked, but here it is:

\[\max_{A_D}(R_t - r_t - d)A_D\] where \(A_D\) is the decision of the capital leasing firms.

4.2.4 Ramsey Goods Market Clearing

\[Y_t = C_t + I_t\]

Note: Asset Market Clearing implies goods market clearing in Ramsey

4.2.5 Def “Competitive (Walrasian) Equilibrium”

Competitive equilibrium is a list of quantities \(\{C_t, Y_t, I_t, K_t, A_t\}\) and prices \(\{w_t, r_t, R_t\}\) such that

  1. \(\{C_t, A_t\}\) solves the household problem given \(\{w_t,r_t\}\)
  2. Production side equilibrium holds for all \(t\)
    1. \(w_t = \frac{\partial Y_t}{\partial L_t}, R_t = \frac{\partial Y_t}{\partial K_t}, r_t = R_t - d\)
  3. Market clearing for all \(t\)
    1. \(A_t = K_t \Rightarrow Y_t = C_t + I_t\)

4.2.6 Def “Production Side Equilibirum”

A list of quantities \(\{C_t, Y_t,K_t\}\) and prices \(\{w_t, r_t, R_t\}\) satisfying, for all \(t\):

  1. Aggregate production function: \(Y_t = F(K_t, L_t)\)
  2. Profit maximization and factor market clearing
    1. Final goods sector: \((K,L) \in argmax_{(K,L)}(F(K,L) - R_tK - w_tL)\) given \((w_t, R_t)\)
    2. Value maximization and asset market clearing, capital leasing: \(K_t \in argmax_{K}(\int_t^\infty e^{-\bar{r}(t,s) - d(s - t)} R_s K ds - K)\) given \((r_t, R_t)\)
  3. Free entry (automatic given profit maximization and market clearing)
    1. \(\max_{(K,L)}(F(K,L) - R_tK - w_tL) = 0\)

4.2.7 Def “Household Agrees with the Planner”

When we have (1) production side equilibrium, and (2) Asset market clearing (\(A = K\)), then planner and household have the same (1) utility, (2) LOM for wealth (\(\dot{K}\)), (3) transversality condition.

Careful: if we don’t have asset market clearing and/or production side equilibrium, HH would have different resources and the stable arms would not coincide.

4.2.8 Def “Rational Expectations Equilibrium (REE)”

Refinement of WE - we add an expectation/belief function \(\Phi(K) = c_t, C_t = \Phi(K_t) L_t\) that allows households to forecast future factor prices

FINISH MEE

4.2.9 Def “Representative Household flow budget constraint”

\[\dot{A_t} = r_tA_t+w_tL_t - C_t\]

4.3 Government and fiscal policy in the Ramsey model

5 Endogenous Growth

  • \(A\): (stock?) patent number (index)

  • \(P_{A,t}\): price of idea

  • \(M\) (?) market value of patents

  • \(\dot{M}\): Capital gain

  • \(\varphi\): Knowledge externality. Stepping on toes or synergy.

  • \(\lambda\): Labor externality…

  • \(\theta\): ???????????????????????????

  • flow = quantity measured as bricks per quarter

  • stock = total number of bricks

6 Misc

On the balanced growth path, all components of expenditure have to grow at the same rate as Y

6.0.1 Def “Euler equation”

An Euler equation is something that relates choice in one period to choice in another period.

6.0.2 Remark “discrete to continuous, stocks vs flows”

REMEMBER HOW TO CONVERT - one of them gets multiplied by delta t

In steady state \(\dot{K} \neq 0\)!!!! ONLY RATIOS ARE UNCHANGING IN STEADY STATES!!

6.1 Prereqs

6.1.1 Def “Annual growth rate”

6.1.2 Def “Annualized growth rate”

6.1.3 Def “Instantaneous growth rate”

6.1.4 Fact “Arithmetic with growth rates”

  • \(g_{XY} = g_X + g_Y\)
  • \(g_{X/Y} = g_X - g_Y\)
  • \(g_{X^\alpha} = \alpha g_X\)

6.1.5 Def “Rules of growth rates”

  • Product rule: \(g_{XY} = g_X + g_Y\)
  • Ratio rule: \(g_{X/Y} = g_X - g_Y\)
  • Power rule : \(g_{X^\alpha} = \alpha g_X\).
